summary: Gruber examines Apple's support page listing power consumption and thermal output for every Mac Mini model from 2005 to the M1 in 2020, highlighting how dramatically the M1 improved efficiency. He notes the 2018 Intel Mac Mini was a thermal outlier — consuming three times the power of the M1 while being slower — and conjectures that Intel's rising thermal footprint was a key reason Apple stalled Mac desktop updates from 2014 to 2017. The piece frames the 'gap years' not as Apple losing interest in the Mac, but as Apple being constrained by Intel's chips running too hot. Gruber closes with the striking observation that the M1 at full load uses roughly the same power as the original G4 Mac Mini at idle.
key_points: The M1 Mac Mini uses 39W max vs. the 2018 Intel model's 122W, a threefold reduction while delivering much higher performance.
The 2014-2017 gap in Mac desktop updates, widely interpreted as Apple losing interest in the Mac, may have been driven by Intel's chips exceeding Apple's thermal standards.
Apple's 2017 roundtable was a mea culpa that acknowledged the Mac Pro and Mini stagnation, but Schiller's tepid Mac Mini comments fueled further doubt.
The M1 Mac Mini at full load consumes roughly the same power as the original 2005 PowerPC G4 Mac Mini sitting idle.
The 2018 Mac Mini, though well-regarded, was a thermal outlier that broke the historical efficiency trendline — a sign of Intel's limitations.
money_quotes: The 2018 Mac Mini has three times the power consumption and thermal output of the new M1 Mac Mini — and much higher numbers than the 2014 Mac Mini models it replaced. It's an outlier on the trendline.

It's not hard to look at this simple table of power consumption and thermal output and consider that those 'gap years' in Mac desktop hardware — the Mac Mini in particular — were to a large extent the result of Intel's chips running way too hot for Apple's standards.
In 2017, Apple bit the bullet and did the best they could with what Intel had to offer, realizing then that they needed another generation of Intel Macs before Apple Silicon Macs would be ready.
A new M1 Mac Mini running at full speed uses about the same power as a G4 Mac Mini did just sitting there with the Finder open doing nothing.
I don't have GeekBench numbers handy for the G4 Mac Mini, but I believe the new M1 models are noticeably faster.
The Mac Mini remains a product in our lineup, but nothing more to say about it today.
key_insight: A simple table of power and thermal specs across Mac Mini generations reveals that Intel's worsening efficiency — not Apple's indifference — likely caused the Mac desktop 'gap years,' and that Apple Silicon has brought the Mac Mini back to efficiency levels not seen since the PowerPC era.
